disease.name: Ischemic Hepatitis (Shock Liver)
disease.note: Acute hepatocyte injury due to reduced hepatic perfusion, typically from systemic hypotension (cardiogenic, hemorrhagic, or septic shock). Characterized by dramatic, rapid elevation of transaminases (often >10,000 IU/L) with rapid normalization upon hemodynamic restoration. Usually subclinical; jaundice is mild and transient. Prognosis determined by underlying cause, not liver injury itself.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Shock unresolved -> ICU; Liver injury resolving -> MONITOR; Pre-existing liver disease + shock -> HIGH risk of decompensation

historyFeatures.note: Any hemodynamic compromise: cardiac arrest, MI, arrhythmia, hemorrhage, sepsis
Encephalopathy from underlying shock or liver failure

examFeatures.note: JVD, peripheral edema, crackles if cardiogenic
If severe or chronic liver disease background

laboratory.name: AST and ALT
laboratory.indication: dramatic elevation (often >10,000 IU/L) within 24h of insult; AST > ALT; rapid decline (50% drop in 72h) if hemodynamics restored

laboratory.name: LDH
laboratory.indication: dramatically elevated; correlates with AST; often >10,000

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: mild elevation (usually <5 mg/dL); appears after AST peak

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: prolonged (synthetic dysfunction); may worsen before improving

laboratory.name: Serum creatinine and electrolytes
laboratory.indication: AKI commonly associated from same underlying shock

laboratory.name: Viral hepatitis serology
laboratory.indication: rule out viral cause if pattern atypical

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.indication: rule out biliary obstruction; assess hepatic vasculature; evaluate for pre-existing liver disease

imaging.name: Echocardiogram
imaging.indication: if cardiogenic shock suspected; assess LV function

management.protocolReference: AASLD Guidelines: Acute Liver Failure; Critical Care Hepatology

pathways.criteria: Ischemic hepatitis from any cause of hemodynamic compromise
pathways.treatment: TREAT THE UNDERLYING CAUSE — this is the most important intervention. Restore hemodynamic stability: fluids, vasopressors, inotropes as indicated. Treat the underlying shock state (cardiogenic, septic, hemorrhagic). Liver-specific treatment is generally not needed. Monitor AST/ALT, INR, bilirubin daily. The transaminases will fall rapidly once perfusion is restored (50% reduction within 48-72h). Prognosis is determined by the underlying condition, not the transaminase elevation.

pathways.criteria: INR >1.5 + encephalopathy, or decompensation of pre-existing cirrhosis
pathways.treatment: ICU management. NAC IV may have benefit in severe cases (extrapolated from DILI, limited evidence in ischemic hepatitis). Monitor for hepatic encephalopathy (lactulose if indicated). Correct coagulopathy only if actively bleeding. Avoid hepatotoxic drugs. Renal replacement therapy if AKI. Transplant evaluation rarely indicated (most recover if underlying shock resolves; prognosis primarily determined by underlying illness).
